skill.name: database-migration
skill.description: Guide for creating idempotent Supabase migrations with RLS policies, workspace isolation, indexes, constraints, and verification steps.
skill.summary: Create repeatable Supabase migrations with workspace-aware RLS patterns.
skill.author: CleanExpo

security_audit.summary: The static analyzer reported many high-risk patterns, but most are false positives caused by markdown code fences, inline SQL examples, and common SQL keywords. The skill is documentation-only, but one sample RLS policy uses a very broad allow-all pattern that could weaken tenant isolation if copied without a service-role-only restriction.

medium_findings.title: Overly Broad RLS Management Policy Example
medium_findings.description: The sample migration creates a policy named "System can manage agents" using FOR ALL with USING true and WITH CHECK true. If copied into a Supabase project without a restrictive role target, this can permit broad table access and undermine workspace isolation.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The SQL is explicit and security-relevant, but it appears in an instructional example rather than executable code. Risk depends on whether users copy it without adding role restrictions.

low_findings.title: Read-Only Local Shell Commands in Documentation
low_findings.description: The only real shell commands are cat and ls commands for reading a schema reference and listing migration files. They are local, read-only, and do not execute remote code or process untrusted input.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command block is visible and limited to local inspection commands. No command substitution, network access, or destructive operation is present.

low_findings.title: Markdown Backticks Misclassified as Ruby Shell Execution
low_findings.description: Multiple static findings identify markdown fences and inline code spans as Ruby or shell backtick execution. These are formatting markers in SKILL.md, not executable Ruby code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed file is markdown and the flagged spans are code fences or inline code examples. There is no Ruby source or runtime context.

low_findings.title: SQL RLS Text Misclassified as Sensitive Windows SAM Access
low_findings.description: The sensitive-data findings point to ALTER TABLE statements that enable row level security. No Windows SAM database path, credential dump command, or Windows registry access is present.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The lines contain PostgreSQL RLS statements, not Windows account database references. The surrounding context is Supabase migration SQL.

low_findings.title: SQL Keywords Misclassified as System Reconnaissance
low_findings.description: The reconnaissance findings are ordinary SQL clauses such as SELECT, INNER JOIN, WHERE, and foreign key references inside migration examples. No host enumeration, process listing, network scanning, or OS discovery is shown.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged content is SQL for tenant filtering and foreign keys. It does not request system information outside the database schema context.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Findings Are Markdown Filename False Positives
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography findings appear to come from words and filenames in documentation, including schema-reference.md and migration naming guidance. No MD5, SHA1, DES, or custom cryptography implementation is present in the reviewed file.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines describe migrations and documentation paths. I did not find evidence of weak cryptographic algorithms in those locations.

dangerous_patterns.title: Allow-All RLS Policy Pattern
dangerous_patterns.description: The documentation includes an RLS policy pattern with FOR ALL USING true WITH CHECK true. This is dangerous if applied to user-accessible roles without explicit service-role scoping.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The pattern is directly present in the SQL example. Its exploitability depends on deployment context and Supabase role targeting.

content.value_statement: Database migrations can break tenant isolation when RLS policies and schema checks are inconsistent. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code a repeatable process for writing idempotent Supabase SQL.
actual_capabilities: Guides creation of timestamped Supabase migration files.
Provides idempotent SQL patterns for tables, indexes, functions, and enum types.
Shows workspace isolation policies using user_organizations and workspaces joins.
Includes examples for constraints, foreign keys, triggers, and comments.
Provides a pre-application checklist for migration review.
limitations: Does not run migrations or connect to Supabase automatically.
Assumes the project uses workspaces and user_organizations tables.
Requires human review before applying policies to production data.
Includes a broad system policy example that should be restricted before use.

use_cases.title: Add a Tenant-Aware Table
use_cases.description: Create a new table with workspace_id, indexes, and row level security before release.

use_cases.title: Standardize Migration Reviews
use_cases.description: Use the checklist to verify idempotency, constraints, comments, and policy coverage.

use_cases.title: Repair RLS Policy Drift
use_cases.description: Compare existing policies against the documented user_organizations and workspaces pattern.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Basic Migration
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner migration for a new workspace table.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the database-migration skill to draft a Supabase migration for a new workspace-scoped table named projects. Include idempotent table creation, an index, and basic RLS.

prompt_templates.title: Review an Existing Migration
prompt_templates.scenario: Check migration quality before applying it.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the database-migration skill to review this Supabase migration for idempotency, workspace isolation, indexes, constraints, and RLS policy risks.

prompt_templates.title: Add Functions and Triggers
prompt_templates.scenario: Extend a schema while keeping repeatable deployments.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the database-migration skill to design a migration that adds an updated_at trigger, a supporting function, and safe rollback notes for a workspace table.

prompt_templates.title: Harden Multi-Tenant Access
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced RLS policy review for production data.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the database-migration skill to redesign these Supabase RLS policies so admin, owner, user, and service-role behavior are explicit and tenant-safe.

output_examples.input: Request to add an agent registry table for each workspace.
output_examples.output: A migration plan with a timestamped filename, table fields, workspace index, RLS policy notes, and a verification checklist.

output_examples.input: Request to review a migration that already exists.
output_examples.output: A structured review that identifies missing idempotency guards, missing indexes, policy gaps, and production review items.

output_examples.input: Request to fix a policy that references workspace_members.
output_examples.output: A replacement policy approach using user_organizations and workspaces, plus notes about expected roles and test cases.

best_practices: Check the current schema and migration history before drafting new SQL.
Use idempotent creation patterns for tables, indexes, functions, triggers, and enum types.
Review every RLS policy for tenant isolation and explicit role intent before production use.
anti_patterns: Do not copy broad FOR ALL policies into production without role restrictions.
Do not create workspace-scoped tables without a workspace_id index and RLS policy.
Do not apply migrations from an AI draft without database-owner review.

faq.question: Does this skill apply migrations automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides guidance and examples. A user must apply SQL through approved Supabase workflows.

faq.question: Which database does it target?
faq.answer: It targets Supabase projects that use PostgreSQL, RLS, workspaces, and user_organizations.

faq.question: Can Claude Code use this skill?
faq.answer: Yes. It is marked for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code compatibility.

faq.question: Is the generated SQL production-ready?
faq.answer: It is a starting point. Review table names, roles, policy targets, and service-role behavior before production use.

faq.question: Why does the audit show medium risk?
faq.answer: One example uses an allow-all RLS management policy. That pattern needs explicit role scoping before use.

faq.question: What should I provide in a prompt?
faq.answer: Provide the desired table, tenant model, roles, required constraints, and any existing migration or schema context.
